What this guide is

Buyers are showing up to every conversation armed with AI. They've already benchmarked your pricing, reviewed your product gaps, flagged your company's risk signals — and filtered your inbox.

This guide shows you exactly how to fight back. Inside, you'll find the five workflows that turn raw account intelligence into a boardroom-ready value story — the kind that gets through AI filters, resonates with CFOs, and creates urgency. Each workflow includes a copy-paste prompt you can use today, and a real example of what Whyzer produces automatically.

The Value Story Framework
1
Get through the inbox
2
Promise a financial outcome
3
Nail the executive priorities
4
Map to personal drivers
5
Pre-empt negative perceptions
01
Deep Account Research in Minutes
Build an analyst-grade financial intelligence brief on any account — in minutes, not days.
Before you write a single word to a buyer, you need intelligence — not just company background, but the financial pressures, strategic gaps, and cost exposure that justify a high-value purchase. This workflow generates an analyst-grade account brief in minutes.
⏱ Manual
60–90 min
10-K filings, earnings transcripts, press releases, G2, and LinkedIn — manually cross-referenced.
⚡ With Whyzer
~3 min
Whyzer generates a structured financial intelligence brief automatically from account data.
Prompt — Deep Account Research
# Copy and run in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini You are a top-tier equity analyst and enterprise sales strategist. Your task is to analyze [COMPANY NAME] and identify the most critical financial pressures, inefficiencies, and strategic risks that could justify a high-value enterprise purchase. Focus ONLY on insights tied to measurable financial impact (revenue, cost, margin, cash flow, or risk). Structure your response: 1. Financial Performance Signals (What's happening?) 2. Balance Sheet & Capital Pressure (Where are they constrained?) 3. Strategic Priorities vs Financial Reality (Where's the gap?) 4. Hidden Cost Drivers & Inefficiencies (Where is money leaking?) 5. Risk Exposure (What could hurt them financially?)
>> Whyzer Example Tableau sells to NVIDIA ⚡ ~3 min
02
Speak the Language of Executives
Translate your message from product language into financial language — the language CFOs actually respond to.
Executives don't speak features. They speak revenue, margin, cost, and risk. This workflow takes your draft message and rewrites it through the lens of a CFO — stripping out product language and replacing it with financial consequence.
⏱ Manual
30–45 min
Multiple draft iterations, peer review, alignment with sales leadership.
⚡ With Whyzer
~1 min
Paste your draft. The CFO rewrite draws on the account intelligence from Step 1 automatically.
Prompt — Executive Language Rewrite
You are a CFO-level executive. Your job is to rewrite my message so it sounds like it belongs in an executive conversation. Critique it: • Where am I using product, feature, or technical language? • Where am I vague or generic? • What would an executive not care about? Rewrite so that it: • Leads with a business problem • Focuses on financial impact (revenue, cost, margin, risk) • Connects to business priorities • Removes unnecessary detail — is clear and concise • Adds consequence: what happens if nothing changes • Feels relevant and timely [PASTE YOUR DRAFT MESSAGE HERE]
>> Whyzer Example Tableau sells to NVIDIA ⚡ ~1 min
03
Point of View Development
Build a boardroom-ready argument for why this account has a problem worth solving now.
A POV is not a pitch. It's a structured argument: this company has a specific problem, it's showing up financially in a specific way, and here's what changes if they act now. This is what earns a spot in a boardroom conversation.
⏱ Manual
2–4 hrs
Synthesizing research into a coherent narrative, multiple revision cycles, internal review.
⚡ With Whyzer
~3 min
Whyzer builds the full POV from your account research with an opportunity score and evidence rating.
Prompt — POV Development
You are a CFO, CEO, and Board Member. Your job is to help me build a boardroom-ready Point of View (POV) that can justify executive attention and investment. Step 1: Based on the research attached (ATTACH YOUR FILES) • What is the most critical business problem? • How is it showing up financially? (revenue, cost, margin, risk) • Why does it matter at the executive level? Step 2: • Estimate the size of the problem (even directionally) • What is the financial consequence if nothing changes? • What metrics would executives care about here? Step 3: Build a clear POV using: • Context — what's happening in the business • Problem — what's broken or at risk • Impact — financial + strategic consequences • Insight — what others are missing or getting wrong • Opportunity — what changes if solved
>> Whyzer Example Tableau sells to NVIDIA ⚡ ~3 min
04
Sharpen the POV Through Multi-Threading
Stress-test your narrative against every stakeholder in the buying committee before you walk in.
Enterprise deals die because one champion doesn't have enough internal pull. Your POV needs to resonate with the CFO, Head of Operations, IT, and the business unit leader — simultaneously. This workflow stress-tests your narrative across all four stakeholders before you walk in.
⏱ Manual
1–2 hrs
Researching each stakeholder's priorities, building persona-specific messaging, internal prep.
⚡ With Whyzer
~2 min
Whyzer maps your POV to each key executive with relevance scores and persona-specific talking points.
Prompt — Multi-Threading
You are acting as multiple stakeholders inside [COMPANY NAME]: • CFO • Head of Operations • Head of IT / Technology • Business Unit Leader I will give you my current Point of View (POV). Your job is to pressure-test and refine it through multi-threading. For each stakeholder: • Do they agree with the problem? Why or why not? • What part feels inaccurate, incomplete, or overstated? • What do they care about more than what I've said? • Where would stakeholders disagree internally? • What tensions or competing priorities exist? • Where is the POV weak in financial clarity? • How can the impact be made more specific or urgent? [PASTE YOUR POV HERE]
>> Whyzer Example Tableau sells to NVIDIA ⚡ ~2 min
05
Create Sales Assets at the Speed of the Deal
Turn your POV into a boardroom-ready visual that works without you in the room.
You have a POV. You've multi-threaded it. Now you need to turn it into something the buyer can hold — an infographic, a one-pager, a pitch deck slide — in minutes. This workflow converts your narrative into boardroom-ready visuals tailored to a specific executive.
⏱ Manual
3–5 hrs
Briefing a designer, copy iterations, approvals, final export — minimum 3 days if external design.
⚡ With Whyzer
~3 min
Select your target executive, color palette, and visual style. Whyzer generates the infographic instantly.
Prompt — Sales Asset Creation
You are a top enterprise seller and executive communications expert. Turn my POV into a high-impact sales asset that makes the problem clear, tangible, and compelling. Build the asset with: 1. A strong headline that states the problem clearly and specifically 2. A short explanation of what's happening (simple, not generic) 3. A visual description that makes the problem tangible 4. A clear financial impact callout (readable in seconds) Guidelines: • Remove generic language — make it specific to this company • It must NOT sound like a product pitch • Keep it simple, scannable, and limited in text • Ensure it works without me explaining it [PASTE YOUR POV HERE]
>> Whyzer Example Tableau sells to NVIDIA ⚡ ~3 min
